The code fix itself is appropriate, idiomatic, and regression-free. _close_all_pools now snapshots _OPEN_POOLS under _init_lock (the same lock under which _register_pool_cleanup appends) and closes outside the lock; since _safe_close never takes _init_lock there is no deadlock. This correctly addresses the concurrent-mutation/free-threaded-reallocation concern.

However, the regression test test_close_all_pools_snapshots_under_lock (tests/test_persistence.py:703) does NOT discriminate the fix. I empirically reverted the fix to direct iteration (for p in _OPEN_POOLS: _safe_close(p)) and ran the test: it PASSED identically (1 passed), exactly as with the fix present. The full suite (70 passed, 2 skipped) is unchanged either way.

Why it fails to discriminate: (1) MutatingPool.close() only APPENDS to _OPEN_POOLS. On CPython, appending to a list during a for-loop does NOT raise "RuntimeError: list changed size during iteration" — that error is raised by dict/set iterators, not list iterators. The test comment cites the wrong mechanism. (2) The appended object() has no .close(), but _safe_close swallows the AttributeError, so nothing surfaces. (3) The genuine hazard the fix guards against — list reallocation UB under a CONCURRENT append from another thread on a free-threaded (GIL-less) build — is single-threaded here and cannot be reproduced on the standard GIL build (env is Python 3.14.5 with GIL enabled).

A discriminating test would need to spy that _close_all_pools acquired _init_lock before iterating (e.g. wrap/instrument the lock, or have close() block while a second thread appends and assert no corruption), or assert the snapshot semantics directly (close() that mutates and verify the iteration covered exactly the original 2 pools). As written, the test would not fail if the fix were reverted, so completeness is not met.

Fix at src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:226-235 wraps future.add_done_callback(_release) in try/except BaseException; on an interrupt landing in the attach window it calls _release(future) to release the bounded-pool slot, then re-raises. This is correct and idiomatic: (1) except BaseException is the right breadth since KeyboardInterrupt/SystemExit (the motivating in-window interrupts) are not Exception subclasses; (2) _release decrements _inflight under _inflight_lock and ignores its future argument, so the explicit call is safe; (3) no double-release risk — if add_done_callback raises, the callback was never registered, so the slot is released exactly once via the except path; (4) the interrupt is preserved for the caller via re-raise. The regression test test_submit_bounded_releases_slot_if_callback_registration_is_interrupted (tests/test_persistence.py:729) DOES discriminate: I reverted the fix to a bare `future.add_done_callback(_release); return future` and the test failed with `assert 1 == 0` (slot leaked); restoring the guard makes it pass. Verified by running pytest with the project venv.

CONCERN — completeness and the accuracy of the justification:

1. The inline comment (line 229) asserts: "_release is idempotent for a given future (the callback runs at most once)." This is FALSE. _release (line 196) does an unconditional `_inflight -= 1` with no per-future guard.

2. There is a real, uncovered double-decrement / over-release path. CPython's Future.add_done_callback (verified via inspect) has two branches: if the future is not yet done it does `_done_callbacks.append(fn); return`. A BaseException (signal) can land AFTER the append but BEFORE the return — the callback is then registered AND will fire when the future completes (decrement #1), while the guard's `except` also calls _release explicitly (decrement #2). Net result: _inflight drops to -1 (over-release), under-counting saturation and allowing >_POOL_WORKERS concurrent calls until it self-corrects.

This residual over-release is materially LESS harmful than the leak it fixes (transient over-subscription that self-heals vs. a permanent pool brick), so the change is a net improvement and introduces no regression worse than the original bug — hence appropriate=true. But the fix does not fully close the symmetric edge, the regression test does not exercise the "callback already registered" path, and the comment's safety rationale is demonstrably incorrect. A complete fix would make _release idempotent per-future (e.g., a one-shot flag/sentinel) so the explicit release and the queued callback cannot both decrement, and correct the comment.

F-005 is a reclassified-false-positive. The original claim was that OtelTracer.span (and the seam's _traced) does not set ERROR status on a raised exception. That claim is FALSE: OTel's start_as_current_span defaults record_exception=True and set_status_on_exception=True, so an escaping exception is auto-recorded as an exception span event and the status is set to StatusCode.ERROR. I verified this empirically against the installed SDK: with defaults, status=ERROR and an "exception" event is present; with record_exception=False/set_status_on_exception=False, status=UNSET and no exception event. The reclassification judgment is therefore correct and well-justified.

Appropriateness: The actual code change is a comment-only addition at both span sites (src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py:206-209 and src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:596-597). No manual error-handling existed to remove — the diff confirms nothing functional was deleted, so there is zero regression risk and the code remains idiomatic (relying on documented SDK defaults rather than redundant manual set_status calls). The seam.py changes in the broader commit (atexit pool shutdown, callback guard, deadline gating) belong to other findings (F-022/F-032/F-043), not F-005.

Completeness: The retained characterization test tests/test_observability.py::test_otel_span_records_error_status_and_exception_on_raise (passes) installs a real in-memory OTel provider, raises inside OtelTracer().span, and asserts spans[0].status.status_code is StatusCode.ERROR AND an "exception" event exists. This is genuinely discriminating: my isolated repro shows that disabling either default (the realistic regression vector — an accidental record_exception=False or an SDK default flip) makes both assertions fail. It correctly pins the SDK guarantee the code depends on. Therefore an exception escaping the `with tracer.start_as_current_span(name)` body in OtelTracer.span (tracing.py:210) and _span (seam.py:598) is automatically recorded as an `exception` span event and the span status is set to ERROR. Per the reclassification mandate (step 3), the relevant bar is whether the judgment is correct and well-justified — it is — not revert-discrimination of a code change.

NO REGRESSION: The git diff (commit 2b07dfd) shows this commit added only a clarifying comment to OtelTracer.span plus an unrelated F-044 log.warning in bootstrap_tracing; the span logic is byte-identical to the prior revision. Full observability suite: 23 passed, 2 skipped. Named test passes.

F-035 is a scoped-decline of a performance finding (numpy vectorization of FeatureMatrix.__post_init__ validation). I judged the DECISION, not merely the code change.

Decline rationale is sound and empirically verified in the project venv:
1. np.asarray([True, 1.0]) yields dtype float64 with the bool coerced to np.float64(1.0) — isinstance(.,bool) is False. So a numpy whole-matrix fast-path would silently accept a Python bool, breaking the #149 bool-exclusion contract. Confirmed.
2. isinstance(np.float64(2.5), float) is True but isinstance(np.float32(2.5), float) is False — the pure-Python isinstance(v,float) check has acceptance semantics no single numpy coercion reproduces. Confirmed. There is genuinely no numpy form preserving both invariants, so keeping the authoritative pure-Python check is the correct call.

The actual code change is purely cosmetic/semantics-preserving: `import math`→`from math import isfinite`, plus hoisting isinstance to a local — no behavioral change, so no regression. The substantive deliverable is the documented decision plus the preserved contract.

The named regression test tests/test_contract.py:161 (test_feature_matrix_rejects_non_numeric_and_non_finite_rows) discriminates the preserved contract on all three pillars: non-numeric "x"→TypeError, bool True→TypeError (the exact invariant the declined numpy remedy would break), NaN→ValueError, plus a positive 1.0 case. It passes on current code (1 passed). Critically, it WOULD FAIL if the declined naive-numpy remedy were applied, since (True,) would coerce to float64 and no longer raise — so the test genuinely guards the decline. Appropriate and complete.

The code change in /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/pipeline.py is APPROPRIATE: run_study gains a verify_trail: bool = True keyword (line 272) gating the end-of-run trail.verify() (lines 702-710). It is minimal, idiomatic, thoroughly documented (the comment correctly notes the skipped re-walk is the redundant in-memory one and does not affect a caller's verify_records on a deserialized trail), and the default stays True so safety is preserved. No regression risk; trail.verify() has only this one call site in the file.

It is NOT COMPLETE on the regression-test dimension. I empirically tested discrimination by mutating the fix:
- if verify_trail: -> if False: (verify never runs) => test_verify_trail_default_still_verifies FAILS (good — it spies ProvenanceTrail.verify via monkeypatch and asserts calls==1, so it pins the default-verifies behavior).
- if verify_trail: -> if True: (flag ignored, verify ALWAYS runs — i.e. the opt-out is completely broken) => test_verify_trail_can_be_opted_out STILL PASSES, both alone and in suite.

The opt-out test only asserts verify_records(res.provenance) succeeds, which is true regardless of whether the in-run re-walk was skipped — it is independent of the flag's effect. So the actual behavior F-036 introduces (skipping the redundant verify when verify_trail=False) is NOT pinned by any test. grep confirms no test asserts verify() is skipped (no calls==0 / spy assertion on the False path). The sibling test already shows the right pattern: a counting monkeypatch on ProvenanceTrail.verify; the opt-out test should do the same and assert calls["n"] == 0 when verify_trail=False. As written, a future refactor that drops the gate would pass CI silently.

deferred-by-mandate judgment is correct and well-justified. Verified the three load-bearing claims: (1) The campaign mandate genuinely requires committing transcripts as provenance — CAMPAIGN.md §7 binds each pass to commit developments/documentation, the observability/provenance dimension treats transcripts as auditable provenance, and an ACTIVE pre-commit hook (git config core.hooksPath=.githooks; /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.githooks/pre-commit + scripts/capture_transcripts.py) snapshots the session into transcripts/sessions/ on every commit. Gitignoring transcripts/ would directly contradict this standing instruction, so declining the proposed fix is the right call. (2) The public-release caveat is already documented in /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/transcripts/README.md under 'Scope & caveats (read before publishing)', including the exact remediation (git rm -r --cached transcripts/ + a .gitignore entry) and the note that transcripts are not shipped in the wheel. (3) The residual exposure (local paths, operator email, reasoning) is mitigated, not ignored: secret redaction is genuinely implemented in scripts/capture_transcripts.py via _SECRET_PATTERNS (sk-ant tokens, GitHub gh*_ tokens, bearer/JWT, OAuth/_TOKEN/_KEY/_SECRET env values). No code change is warranted, so the lack of a regression test is appropriate (n/a is correct). The only residual is the documented/accepted tradeoff that a future public release must heed the README caveat — which is the explicit governance decision under the current private-node mandate, not an unaddressed gap.

F-035 is a scoped-decline: a numpy fast-path for FeatureMatrix.__post_init__ validation was evaluated and deliberately rejected, retaining the pure-Python authoritative check (micro-optimized with hoisted _isinst/isfinite locals). I empirically verified both decline claims under py3.14+numpy: (1) np.asarray([True,1.0]) yields float64, silently upcasting bool to numeric — so a numpy path cannot enforce the bool-exclusion contract (#149); (2) isinstance(np.float64(3.14), float) is True, which the pure-Python check accepts and a numpy dtype check would not preserve identically. There is genuinely no numpy form holding both invariants, so the decline judgment is correct and well-justified; the comment is also honest about the scope caveat. The retained micro-opt (local binding) is idiomatic and behavior-preserving (file is git-clean). Discrimination: tests/test_contract.py::test_feature_matrix_rejects_non_numeric_and_non_finite_rows asserts exactly the protected invariants (bool->TypeError, nan->ValueError, non-numeric->TypeError, valid float constructs). It passes as-is, and when I simulated the rejected numpy-upcast behavior by removing the bool check, the test FAILED (DID NOT RAISE TypeError), proving it discriminates the load-bearing contract. Minor non-blocking nit: the rationale's phrasing 'reject numpy float scalars' is loose since np.float32 is not a float subclass while np.float64 is, but the conclusion is sound.

Fix in /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/pipeline.py adds a keyword-only `verify_trail: bool = True` param (line 272) and gates the redundant end-of-run `trail.verify()` behind `if verify_trail:` (lines 702-710). Default stays True, preserving the safety net. APPROPRIATE: the `run_end` terminal entry (line 717) and the trail itself are recorded unconditionally — opting out skips ONLY the O(N) re-walk of an in-memory trail the run just built, not provenance integrity. The inline comment honestly scopes the tradeoff and notes external/deserialized verification (caller's verify_records on from_records) is unaffected. Idiomatic, keyword-only, no regression.

COMPLETE / discrimination verified by mutation: replacing `if verify_trail:` with `if False:` makes test_verify_trail_default_still_verifies FAIL (it pins exactly 1 trail.verify() call by default via a counting monkeypatch). test_verify_trail_can_be_opted_out exercises the verify_trail=False branch end-to-end and asserts the produced chain still re-verifies (verify_records). I empirically confirmed the opt-out actually works: verify_trail=True -> 1 verify() call, verify_trail=False -> 0 calls, chain still valid. Both named tests pass on the pristine file (restored via git checkout after mutation testing).

Minor note (not blocking): the opt-out test asserts chain validity but does not itself pin that verify() was skipped, so a hypothetical no-op opt-out would still pass it; however the default test pins the on-by-default invariant and the opt-out branch is empirically proven to skip the re-walk without corrupting the chain. Coverage is adequate. Verdict CONFIRMED.

The "keep transcripts committed rather than gitignore them" half of the judgment is defensible: transcripts/README.md, .githooks/pre-commit, and capture_transcripts.py establish an intentional, documented provenance design where every commit snapshots the session transcript. Gitignoring transcripts/ wholesale would gut that design, and the public-release caveat is genuinely already documented (transcripts/README.md lines 24-30; capture_transcripts.py docstring lines 11-13). So declining the bare "add a .gitignore entry" remedy is reasonable.

However, labeling the WHOLE finding "deferred-by-mandate" is incomplete and the justification is overstated on three points:

1. Mandate wording is loose. I checked docs/hardening/CAMPAIGN.md, the memory files (hardening-campaign.md, autonomous-operation.md), CHARTER.md and GOVERNANCE.md — none literally mandates "capture transcripts into the repo on every commit." That is a repo design choice (transcripts/README + hook), not the verbal campaign mandate. The CHARTER/GOVERNANCE "append-only provenance" refers to the engine's runtime ProvenanceTrail (ADR-0010), a different mechanism. The substance holds, but the cited basis is imprecise.

2. The deferral conflates "don't gitignore" with "do nothing." Both pass-3 verifiers CONFIRMED F-024 as a genuine privacy concern (1422 tracked files; ~6230 occurrences of "[REDACTED]"; operator email [REDACTED] present; /Users/[REDACTED] paths throughout). A mandate-compatible middle path exists and was not taken: capture_transcripts.py already has _scrub() (lines 48-56) redacting credential-shaped secrets on capture; extending it to redact the email handle and normalize /Users/[REDACTED] -> /Users/[REDACTED] would preserve full provenance value while closing the PII exposure, with NO .gitignore. The deferral presents a false binary (gitignore vs keep) and ignores redaction.

3. The finding's distinct S-5 sub-component is dropped entirely: capture_transcripts.py:29 hardcodes DEFAULT_SRC = ~/.claude/projects/-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc — i.e., hc-assay's capture defaults to a DIFFERENT production system's (hc-grc) Claude directory, risking bleed of unrelated internal reasoning into this repo's public history. That is a real, separable bug independent of the provenance philosophy, conflicts with no mandate, and has no fix or regression guard.

Net: the partial judgment (keep transcripts) is sound, but classifying the entire finding as deferred-by-mandate silently abandons an available, mandate-compatible PII-redaction mitigation and an unrelated cross-project hardcoded-path bug (S-5). Under-justified and incomplete.
